text: Right thoracoscopic diaphragmatic placation. Our patient is a fifty-four-year-old male who presented to his primary care provider complaining of a six-month history of increased dyspnea on exertion. A chest x-ray was obtained and it demonstrated an elevated right hemidiaphragm. Subsequent diaphragmatic fluoroscopy demonstrated paradoxical movement of the diaphragm. The patient was subsequently referred for diaphragmatic placation.

Two standard thoracoscopic ports are visible in the right hemithorax. The surgeon inspects the area to identify the redundant diaphragm. A linear clamp is placed across the diaphragm, and a linear stapler is positioned beneath the clamp to advance the staple line medially. The cross clamp placement is adjusted after each staple load to ensure proper tension and contour of the diaphragm placation.

The surgeon completes the staple line medially and removes the specimen. The peritoneal surface of the diaphragm is inspected, and the staple line is over-sewn. The placation is inspected to ensure proper tension and contour.
